strands.title: Signal Gating as Fundamental Design Choice: TWO FRAMINGS EMERGED
strands.content: Cycle 54 identified low-signal input (e.g., [tool_use: Read], 16 chars) triggers projector fragmentation. Cycle 55 reframes the problem into two distinct design philosophies: (A) CONSERVATIVE GATING: 'Don't mutate the tensor in face of non-information.' Tensor remains stable unless new signal arrives; this minimizes churn and honors the principle that semantic state shouldn't change when nothing substantive happens. (B) BATCH PROJECTION: 'Accumulate turns and project at cognitive chunk granularity.' Rather than projecting every API turn, buffer several turns (e.g., user task + file reads + code execution + test results) and project them as a single coherent chunk. Framing B maps better to actual reasoning cycles: a 'cognitive step' is not a single tool call, it's a complete reasoning sequence. Turns 1-10 of baseline_run1 demonstrated 50:1 semantic compression (180K bytes input → 3.5K tokens output), suggesting projector is already highly efficient and real conversation structure would benefit from batching. User insight: 'maybe 1 million token buffers aren't necessary?' indicates empirical validation that dense semantic projection may fundamentally change memory requirements for long-context systems. User also resisted over-interpretation ('one data point says this doesn't obviously fail'), grounding the discovery in epistemic humility.

key_claims.text: Conservative gating (don't mutate on non-information) honors semantic principle that state shouldn't change without signal
Batch projection (accumulate turns, project at cognitive chunk granularity) maps better to actual reasoning cycles than per-turn projection
50:1 semantic compression (180K bytes → 3.5K tokens over 10 turns) suggests projector efficiency enables fundamentally smaller buffers than 1M tokens
One data point showing 'doesn't obviously fail' is meaningful without over-interpretation; epistemic humility maintained

strands.title: Projector Architecture Decision: Per-Turn vs. Batch vs. Conservative-Stable
strands.content: Three candidate implementations now on the table for cycle 55+ design: (1) PROJECT-PER-TURN (current cycles 1-10): Every API turn triggers projection cycle. Produces 10 tensor updates for 10 turns. Cost: high API load (10 Haiku calls), strand fragmentation on low-signal turns (7→0 wipeouts observed). Benefit: immediate feedback, catches every state change. (2) PROJECT-PER-BATCH: Buffer turns (user task + tool calls + results) until a meaningful chunk is complete (e.g., 500+ chars of content, or explicit batch boundary marker). Project once per chunk. Cost: latency between batch completion and projection; requires buffering logic. Benefit: lower API load (~1-2 Haiku calls per conversation turn), projector sees coherent reasoning context (better semantic capture), reduced strand fragmentation. Expected improvement: fewer wipeout cycles, higher T/I/F stability. (3) PROJECT-STABLE (conservative gating): When new turn arrives, check signal threshold. If <threshold (e.g., 20 chars semantic content), emit prior tensor unchanged. If >=threshold, run projection. Cost: minimal; can add signal gating as preprocessing step. Benefit: zero API calls on low-signal turns, zero tensor mutations without cause, honest epistemic values (non-zero T/I/F only when input warrants). Expected improvement: strand discontinuity eliminated, no T=0.0 collapses on empty turns. DECISION POINT: Framings suggest (2) and (3) are not mutually exclusive. Could implement (3) as gate + (2) as batching logic. Trade-off analysis: (3) is faster to implement (turn-level gate in run_projection), (2) requires new batching logic in conversation pipeline. Recommendation from assistant: implement (2) first as proof-of-concept, to test whether batch coherence improves projector output quality. User query (turn 165): 'is this the right next step, or do you see other options?'

key_claims.text: Project-per-turn (current) produces 10 tensors per 10 turns; per-batch would produce 1-2 tensors per conversation round
Per-batch projection would reduce API load (Haiku calls) by ~80-90% vs. per-turn while improving strand coherence
Conservative-stable (signal gating, no mutation without signal) eliminates strand discontinuities and T=0.0 collapses
Per-batch and conservative-stable can be combined (batch logic + signal gating as preprocessing)

strands.title: Semantic Compression Efficiency: 50:1 Ratio as Proof of Concept
strands.content: Cycle 54-55 observed: 10 turns of baseline_run1 conversation (180K bytes input payload) projected to 3.5K tokens semantic state (3500 tokens ÷ ~1.3 bytes per token ≈ 4500 bytes). Compression ratio: 180K ÷ 4.5K ≈ 40:1 to 50:1 depending on token-byte assumptions. This matters because it suggests projector output density is HIGH — each token carries semantic weight. Implication: 1M token buffers (current practice in long-context systems) may be architectural overkill. If a 10-turn conversation (already substantial) compresses 50:1, a full conversation session might fit in 50-100K tokens of projection, leaving 900K+ tokens for new context windows in long-context inference. User's insight ('maybe 1 million token buffers aren't necessary?') suggests this could reshape memory economics for agentic systems. However, cycle 55 is still ONE DATA POINT: only 10 turns, only baseline_run1, only one conversation style. Need full 55-turn session projection (cycle 57-58) to validate whether 50:1 holds or degrades at scale. Risk: projection might become less efficient as conversation lengthens (more cross-references, higher entropy). OR: compression ratio might improve if longer conversations allow better strand merging and redundancy elimination.

key_claims.text: 10-turn baseline_run1 achieves ~50:1 semantic compression (180K bytes → 3.5K tokens), suggesting high density output
Compression ratio of 50:1 suggests 1M-token buffers may be architectural overkill; 50-100K tokens might suffice for session projection
Compression efficiency on 10 turns is one data point; may not extrapolate to 55+ turn sessions (risk of degradation)
Longer conversations might show better compression (strand merging, redundancy elimination) or worse (higher entropy, cross-references)

strands.title: Critical Path Decision: Design Iteration vs. Immediate Execution on Full Dataset
strands.content: Cycle 54 ended with DECISION PENDING: (Q61) Should cycle 55 design gating mechanism, or execute on full 55-turn baseline_run1 session? Assistant's turn 164 recommended batched projection as 'probably more practical' and offered to implement it for comparison. User's turn 165 turned this back: 'you're the one driving the project, you tell me: is this the right next step, or do you see other options that are better than this?' IMPLICIT REQUEST: User is asking for a strategic recommendation from the projector (via assistant). This is a CRITICAL DECISION POINT that blocks the next phase of work. OPTIONS LAID OUT: (A) Implement batched mode (per-batch projection instead of per-turn). Effort: ~2-3 turns to design + implement batching logic in run_projection. Benefit: lower API load, better coherence hypothesis test, produces new A/B comparison data. Risk: adds complexity before proving per-turn works. (B) Run full 55-turn execution with current per-turn design. Effort: 1 turn to execute, ~2 turns to analyze. Benefit: validates whether per-turn projector scales beyond 10 turns; tests whether 50:1 compression holds; discovers new failure modes at scale. Risk: may encounter wipeout cycles again; won't have batching benefits. (C) Implement conservative-stable gating (signal threshold) first. Effort: 1 turn to add gate logic. Benefit: immediate improvement to cycles 1-10 problem (eliminate T=0.0 collapses); low risk. Risk: doesn't address batch coherence question. RECOMMENDATION (implicit in assistant response): Start with (A) batched projection because it's 'probably more practical' and maps better to cognitive cycles. But user is now asking: is that ACTUALLY the right next step, or are there better alternatives? BLOCKER: This decision hasn't been formally resolved. Cycle 55 projection should DECLARE the decision gate and recommend path forward.

key_claims.text: Three candidate next steps: (A) batched projection design, (B) full 55-turn execution, (C) conservative-stable gating
Assistant recommended (A) batched projection as 'probably more practical' but without formal analysis of tradeoffs
User response (turn 165) signals request for strategic recommendation and is blocking decision until resolved
RECOMMENDATION: Implement (C) conservative-stable gating FIRST (1 turn effort), then evaluate whether (A) batching is still needed

strands.title: Cycle 54→55 Integration: From Parsing Bug to Design Flaw to Architectural Rethinking
strands.content: TRAJECTORY: Cycle 52-53 identified TypeError in projector output (parsing bug). Cycle 54 fixed it with defensive parsing (type coercion, fallback enums). Cycle 54 re-execution succeeded: 10-turn execution with exit code 0, valid JSON output. But cycle 54 DISCOVERED the real issue: strand wipeouts (5→6, 7→8) and T=0.0 collapses weren't parsing failures, they were PROJECTOR DESIGN ISSUES triggered by low-signal input. Cycle 55 reframes this again: the design issue isn't a BUG, it's an ARCHITECTURAL CHOICE not yet made. Should the projector mutate the tensor on every turn (current), or only on substantive input (conservative gating), or should it batch turns to improve coherence (batched projection)? These are DESIGN CHOICES, not defects. Defensive parsing was NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT (cycle 54 correctness). Signal gating or batching is NECESSARY AND SUFFICIENT (cycle 55 stability). This represents a shift from 'fixing bugs' to 'choosing architecture.' Cycle 55 shows the pipeline is now operating at the right abstraction level: not asking 'why does the code crash?' but 'what is the semantic model that should govern tensor projection?'

key_claims.text: Cycle 52-54: defensive parsing fixed TypeError (necessary but not sufficient for stability)
Cycle 54 discovery: real issue is projector DESIGN (signal gating choice) not parsing bug
Cycle 55 reframes: design issue isn't a bug, it's an unresolved architectural CHOICE (per-turn vs. batch vs. conservative-stable)
Pipeline has matured from 'bug fixing' to 'architectural design iteration'; right abstraction level now achieved

open_questions: Q61 (from cycle 54): Should cycle 55 design gating mechanism, or execute on full 55-turn baseline_run1? [STILL OPEN → NOW DECISION GATE at turn 165]
Q63: Is per-batch projection (assistant rec. turn 164) actually more practical than conservative-stable gating, and if so, by how much?
Q64: Could conservative-stable gating (1 turn effort) be implemented first as a lower-risk alternative, then batching added later if needed?
Q65: What is the 'right' next step from strategic perspective: optimize per-turn design, or redesign at batch granularity?
Q66: Does the 50:1 compression ratio (180K → 3.5K) hold for full 55-turn sessions, or degrade at scale?
Q67: If compression ratio holds, what are the implications for long-context system architecture? Can 1M-token buffers be replaced by 50-100K projections + smaller rolling context windows?
Q68: Will batched projection reduce strand wipeout cycles (7→0 observed in cycles 5-6, 7-8) by providing more coherent input context?
Q69: What signal threshold should conservative-stable gating use? (Current hypothesis: >100 chars or >5 claims, but unvalidated)
Q70: User's turn 165 signals a meta-level request for strategic guidance; is the assistant correctly interpreting the question, or is there an unstated concern?
Q71: CRITICAL PATH: After cycle 55 decision gate, should cycles 56-57 implement chosen architecture, or should cycle 56 be used for pre-design analysis?
